schools.summary: Cornelius, a centurion of the Italian band at Caesarea (the Roman capital), a Gentile officer yet devout; the verse opens the epochal account of the conversion of the Gentiles — the door of faith opened to the uncircumcised, the barrier broken.
Henry: the great turning-point at which the gospel breaks the Jew/Gentile barrier; Cornelius the providential link, a Gentile of pure life against whom the Jews could not object.

themes: a certain man in Caesarea called Cornelius
a centurion of the Italian band
the door opened to the Gentiles

schools.summary: Cornelius a God-fearer/proselyte of the gate (a Gentile worshipping in the synagogue without circumcision, Robertson, Clarke), the connecting link between Jew and Gentile; devout, instructing his house, giving alms, praying always.
Calvin: the pattern of a godly life (faith the foundation of all virtue); his family religion praised; the warmth of this Gentile's piety rebuking the coldness of Christians.

themes: a devout man, one that feared God
the God-fearer / proselyte of the gate
the pattern of a godly life

schools.summary: A clear waking vision (not a dream or trance, Wesley, Ellicott) at the ninth hour (evening prayer) while Cornelius was at his devotions — the vision answering his prayer; the angel marking the importance of the occasion (Barnes).
Calvin: the daylight character of the vision given to remove all suspicion of illusion.

themes: he saw in a vision evidently

schools.summary: The prayers and alms a 'memorial' — accepted as a true sacrifice (sacrificial language, Ellicott); the works wrought by grace already working, Cornelius believing in the Light, having the justifying faith of Hebrews 11:6 (Ellicott); a warning against the merely moral misusing his example (Barnes).

Calvin strongly rejects the Papist reading of works as preparation or merit — Cornelius was regenerate by the Spirit, his works the fruits of a faith already given; he hoped for the promised Redeemer though not yet clearly known.
Wesley: every one who seeks faith in Christ should seek it in prayer and doing good; Cornelius accepted, yet still needing the gospel.

debates.question: How could Cornelius's prayers and alms be accepted before clear faith in Christ?

sides.position: They were the fruits of grace and faith already working (in the Light/promised Redeemer), not meritorious preparation
Works as a meritorious preparation anticipating grace (the Papist reading, rejected)

themes: thy prayers and alms come up for a memorial
works as fruits of grace not merit

schools.summary: Calvin: the great lesson that God uses human ministry — the angel does not teach Cornelius but sends him to Peter (as Ananias to Paul), honouring the ministry of preaching; faith comes by hearing (Romans 10:17); those who despise preaching for private revelations are punished.
The angel directs Cornelius to send for Peter at Joppa; God's favour in bringing the apostle to him rather than requiring his journey.

themes: an apostle, not an angel, to teach

schools.summary: The precise direction (Simon the tanner's house by the sea) confirming the divine origin of the vision; 'what thou oughtest to do' (cf. 11:14) showing Cornelius's seeking for the way of salvation (Clarke); the providential placing of Peter at the tanner's house (9:43).

themes: he lodgeth with Simon a tanner
he shall tell thee what thou oughtest to do

schools.summary: Cornelius's prompt obedience; the 'devout soldier' and household servants sharing his piety (Ellicott, Barnes) — piety flourishing even in a camp, the fruit of his household religion.

Calvin: no calling excuses us from worshipping God purely — the soldier's life adorned with godliness; these devout soldiers, embracing Christ, did not forsake their calling, refuting those who deny Christians may bear arms.

themes: he called two household servants and a devout soldier

schools.summary: Cornelius explained the whole vision to his messengers (that they might go cheerfully and relate it faithfully, Gill) and sent them — the obedience of faith; the providential parallel: from Joppa Jonah was sent to the Gentiles at Nineveh, now Peter to Caesarea (Barnes, Clarke).

themes: he declared all these things and sent them to Joppa

schools.summary: As the messengers drew near, Peter went up on the housetop (the flat Eastern roof) to pray at the sixth hour (noon) — the providential timing of the two halves of God's plan meeting precisely (Henry); Peter's habit of prayer again displayed.

themes: Peter went up upon the housetop to pray

schools.summary: Peter fell into a trance (ecstasy) while hungry — the senses suspended, the soul absorbed in the vision (Barnes, Robertson); the hunger suiting the symbolism of the sheet (Wesley), the trance arising in part from the fast and prayer (Ellicott).
Calvin: the trance lifted Peter's mind above earthly things to receive the heavenly oracle.

themes: the vision suited to his state

schools.summary: The great sheet knit at the four corners — the emblem of the universe and its nations, the four corners to which the gospel was to extend (Clarke, recalling Isaiah 11:12); the descent from heaven intimating the divine origin of the religion abolishing the Jew/Gentile distinction (Barnes); 'as it had been' marking a representation, not a literal object.

themes: heaven opened, a great sheet descending
the divine origin of the new religion

schools.summary: The sheet contained clean and unclean animals together (Clarke, Robertson) — the mingling the emblem of the union of Jew and Gentile.

Calvin: as the distinction between clean and unclean animals is abolished, so the Jew/Gentile distinction — God making the covenant of life common to all the world, breaking the middle wall (Ephesians 2:14).

themes: all manner of beasts, clean and unclean
the union of Jew and Gentile

schools.summary: Clarke prefers the sacrificial sense of 'kill' — the clean and unclean animals (Jew and Gentile) to be offered as a spiritual sacrifice through Peter's ministry, opening the door to the Gentiles.
Calvin prefers the plain sense — the abrogation of the food laws, teaching that God rejects no people; the voice from heaven marking the command as divine.

themes: the command to eat the unclean
the abrogation of the food laws

schools.summary: Peter's refusal a polite one with a reason (Robertson), strikingly in character with his impulsive resistance elsewhere (Ellicott, cf. Matthew 16:22); he pleaded his lifelong observance of the ceremonial law (Leviticus 11, Barnes); his obstinacy and turmoil at a command overturning his training (Robertson).
Calvin: the apparent conflict between the law and the vision, which Peter would not resolve hastily, desiring his doubt dissolved before departing from the law.

themes: I have never eaten anything common or unclean
the conflict of law and vision

schools.summary: The immediate sense removed the clean/unclean food distinction, but the higher design was to teach that the Gentiles, esteemed unclean, might now be admitted as the Jews — the middle wall broken (Ephesians 2:14, Barnes); ceremonial laws passing away when the separation ceased.

Calvin extends the principle against all human prohibitions of what God allows (the Papist food laws) — God alone is judge of what is clean; what God has cleansed (the Gentiles) Peter may not call common.

debates.question: Does the vision concern only food laws, or chiefly the Jew/Gentile barrier?

sides.position: Chiefly the higher design — the breaking of the Jew/Gentile wall, the food law abolished incidentally

themes: what God hath cleansed, call not thou common
God alone judges what is clean
the church opened to all nations

schools.summary: The threefold repetition for confirmation, impressing its certainty (Barnes, cf. Genesis 41:32), perhaps echoing 'Feed my sheep' (Ellicott); needed because of Peter's deep scruple and obstinacy, unconvinced until thrice confirmed (Robertson).
Calvin: the repetition confirmed Peter lest doubt remain — showing how deeply the law was rooted; the sheet's return to heaven certifying the message came from God.

themes: the vessel received up into heaven

schools.summary: As Peter pondered the vision (more puzzled than ever, Robertson), the messengers arrived — the providential arrangement of events to fit each other, the event arriving to relieve his perplexity (Barnes); Clarke marvels at the exact convergence and cautions against misreading providence where vision and event do not correspond.

themes: Peter doubted what the vision should mean
the men from Cornelius stood before the gate

schools.summary: The messengers called out and inquired for Peter (Robertson) — completing their errand, having found the obscure tanner's house, asking for him by the name the angel gave Cornelius (v.5); the hinge of the converging providence.

themes: whether Simon Peter were lodged there

schools.summary: As Peter pondered, the Spirit said directly 'Behold, three men seek thee' — the convergence of inner vision and outer event, together the key to God's will (Wesley); the Spirit's word resolving his perplexity by directing him to the men (Robertson).

themes: the direct guidance of the Spirit
vision, event, and inward word agreeing

schools.summary: The Spirit commanded Peter to go 'doubting nothing' — without wavering (Robertson), walking by faith though not yet seeing the full purpose (Ellicott); 'I have sent them' the Spirit owning the mission, the surest warrant.
Calvin: the obedience of faith — God's will, once known, ends all disputation; we must follow with a quiet and constant mind.
Peter, directed by the Spirit, went down at once and asked their errand (Clarke) — his prompt obedience showing he now understood the vision through the call from a Gentile.
Calvin: the wisdom of submitting to God's authority — the vision ('call not thou common') having corrected Peter's judgment, so he goes to a Gentile he would once have counted profane.

themes: Peter went down to the men
what is the cause wherefore ye are come

schools.summary: The threefold commendation of Cornelius — just, God-fearing, of good report among all the Jews (Robertson); Luke's policy of conciliation, guarding against offence by Jewish attestation of his character (Ellicott); warned by an angel.
Calvin: the commendation given that Peter might the less abhor a Gentile so approved, and know the call was of divine origin.

themes: Cornelius, a just man, one that feareth God
of good report among all the Jews
warned from God by an angel

schools.summary: Peter lodged the messengers (hospitality after their journey, a step over the barrier, Chrysostom) and went with them and six brethren (11:12) — the brethren as witnesses, that the great transaction might be established on multiple testimony (Clarke, Ellicott) and vindicate Peter before the Jerusalem church (Robertson).

themes: called he them in, and lodged them

schools.summary: Cornelius waited eagerly (intent on this one thing, setting aside business, Wesley, Robertson) and gathered his kinsmen and near friends — the assembling of the first Gentile congregation to hear the gospel.
Calvin: Cornelius's love for others' salvation — he would not keep the blessing to himself but gathered others to share it, in humility as a fellow-learner.

themes: called together his kinsmen and friends

schools.summary: Cornelius fell at Peter's feet in homage — civil respect in intent (Barnes, Robertson), yet an excess Peter would refuse (v.26); Ellicott notes the contrast with the ceremonial of Peter's supposed successors, and the parallel of John worshipping the angel (Revelation 22:8-9).

Calvin: an immoderate reverence offered to an apostle, an excess touching on the divine; used against the Papist worship of saints and the latria/dulia distinctions — no worship with religion annexed leaves God his honour untouched.

themes: fell down at his feet, and worshipped him
the honour due to God alone

schools.summary: Peter lifted Cornelius up and refused the homage — 'I myself also am a man'; a standing rebuke to undue veneration, in contrast with the ceremonial of his supposed successors (Ellicott, Wesley), paralleling the angel's 'worship God' (Revelation 22:9) and Paul's 'we also are men' (Acts 14:15).
Chrysostom: the apostles teach, before all else, not to think great things of them — apostolic humility.

themes: Stand up; I myself also am a man

schools.summary: Peter, talking with Cornelius, entered and found the many Gentiles assembled — an expectant group eager for his word (Robertson); the conversation perhaps leading to the mutual statement of how they were brought together (Ellicott); Peter's decisive step over the barrier (cf. v.28).

themes: as he talked with him, he went in

schools.summary: The prohibition came from rabbinic custom, not the law itself (Robertson, Ellicott); 'God hath shewed me' to call no man common — humanity redeemed by the Incarnation, no longer unclean (Ellicott, cf. 'Honour all men,' 1 Peter 2:17); the middle wall (Ephesians 2:14) broken.
Calvin: the wall of separation pulled down, the covenant of life now common to both — those made partakers of God's adoption not to be counted aliens.

themes: unlawful for a Jew to keep company with another nation
God hath shewed me not to call any man common

schools.summary: Peter came 'without gainsaying' (his scruples overcome by the Spirit's command, Robertson) and asked the purpose — though he knew it, that Cornelius might tell the story for the company's benefit and his own impression (Wesley, Clarke).
Calvin: the holy silence of faith — receiving God's command meekly without murmuring; Peter's coming 'without gainsaying' the model of obedient faith.

themes: came I unto you without gainsaying
for what intent ye have sent for me

schools.summary: Cornelius recounted his vision — fasting and praying at the ninth hour, an angel ('a man in bright clothing') appeared (Ellicott reconstructs the four-day timeline); the bright clothing the token of heavenly glory (Matthew 28:3).
Calvin: the fasting marked the earnestness of his prayer and guarded the vision against the charge of fantasy; Cornelius modestly calls the angel 'a man.'

themes: four days ago I was fasting and praying
a man stood before me in bright clothing
the devout seeker met by God

schools.summary: The angel's assurance (repeated from v.4) that Cornelius's prayer was heard and alms remembered; the singular 'thy prayer' pointing to a definite request for fuller light (Ellicott); Clarke's lesson of improving grace — he used the light he had, and God increased it.

themes: the answer to the devout seeker

schools.summary: Cornelius recounted the angel's precise direction — send to Joppa for Simon Peter at the tanner's house, 'who shall speak unto thee' (Robertson); the repetition (from vv.5-6) confirming the meeting was ordered by God, down to the name and place.

themes: send to Joppa, call Simon Peter

schools.summary: Cornelius thanks Peter for coming (the idiom of heartfelt gratitude, commending his courage in breaking Jewish custom, Robertson) and declares the company present 'before God, to hear all things commanded thee of God' — the prepared soil for the gospel.

Calvin: the right disposition of hearers — receiving the word as God's, not a mortal man's, in reverence, ready to obey all; the perfect readiness of faith that embraces the whole word, distinguishing God (the author) from man (the minister).

themes: thou hast well done that thou art come
we are all here present before God
ready to hear all commanded of God

schools.summary: Peter opened his sermon with the great truth he now perceived — 'God is no respecter of persons'; acceptance depends not on descent or privilege but on the heart (Barnes); a Gentile need not become a Jew to be a Christian (Robertson, echoing Romans 2:11) — the doctrinal foundation of the Gentile mission.
Henry: 'opened his mouth' marks the solemnity and freedom with which Peter spoke on this great occasion.

themes: God is no respecter of persons
the charter of the Gentile mission

schools.summary: In every nation the God-fearer is accepted — no nation excluded (Robertson); but the works are evidence of heart-piety, not the meritorious ground (Barnes); the God-fearer accepted in Christ the true Light, gaining by conversion a fuller knowledge and blessedness (Ellicott).
Calvin: not justification by works — the first acceptance is free, by grace; the works of the regenerate please God only through faith in Christ, which borrows what works lack.

Wesley: accepted 'through Christ, though he knows him not'; Clarke's strong caution — those in gospel lands cannot plead this verse, God requiring faith and righteousness according to the gospel's light.

debates.question: Does this verse teach that sincere persons of any religion are saved by works/morality apart from Christ?

sides.position: No — accepted by grace through faith (in the Light/Christ), the works being evidence not the ground; no nation excluded
Those in gospel lands cannot plead it — God requires faith and righteousness according to the gospel's light

themes: in every nation he that feareth him is accepted
the equity and love of God

schools.summary: The word God sent to Israel, 'preaching peace by Jesus Christ' — reconciliation between God and men (Robertson, cf. Ephesians 2:17); the triumphant parenthesis 'he is Lord of all' the ground of the Gentile mission, Christ Lord of both Jew and Gentile and no mere prophet (Robertson, Ellicott).
Calvin: peace means the reconciling of men and God, the sum of the church's salvation, with Christ as the pledge.

Peter appealed to what the Gentiles knew — the matter published throughout Judea, beginning from Galilee after John's baptism (Barnes); his outline following the gospel pattern (like Mark, modeled on Peter's preaching, Robertson); building from the known to the unknown (Clarke).

themes: began from Galilee after John's baptism

schools.summary: God anointed Jesus with the Spirit and power (setting him apart as Messiah, proved by his works, Barnes, Clarke); 'went about doing good and healing all oppressed of the devil' the sublime record of his life (Barnes, Robertson); 'God was with him' a lowly term, no low Christology but sparing speech to these hearers (Robertson, Wesley).
Calvin: the anointing furnished Christ with the power of the Spirit; the miracles attesting his divine commission, alluring the world with goodness and grace.

themes: God anointed Jesus with the Holy Ghost and power
healing all oppressed of the devil
the gospel portrait of the Savior

schools.summary: Peter appealed to the apostles as eyewitnesses of all Jesus did (as at Pentecost, 2:32, Robertson); the crucifixion laid to the rulers and people of Jerusalem (Ellicott); 'hanged on a tree' recalling the curse of the law (Deuteronomy 21:23; Galatians 3:13).
Calvin: the apostles both historical witnesses (eyewitnesses) and appointed witnesses (commissioned to testify); Peter touches the death briefly but dwells on the resurrection.

themes: we are witnesses of all things which he did
whom they slew and hanged on a tree

schools.summary: God raised Jesus the third day (ascribed to the Father, fulfilling the Scriptures, Gill, 1 Corinthians 15:4) and showed him openly — manifestly, beyond doubt (Barnes); the third day the divinely chosen interval, proving his death yet barring the charge of imposture (Clarke); the heart of the gospel and God's vindication of Christ.

themes: God raised him the third day
God's vindication of the crucified Christ

schools.summary: The risen Christ shown to chosen, appointed witnesses (not all the people) who ate and drank with him — God's wisdom in unimpeachable witnesses avoiding tumult (Clarke); the 'historian's candour' adding credibility (Robertson, Paley); the eating and drinking the crucial proof against phantom or illusion (Barnes, Luke 24:41-43).
Calvin marvels at Christ's condescension in eating to confirm the disciples' faith — the resurrection proved by many firm testimonies.

themes: not to all the people, but to chosen witnesses
did eat and drink with him after he rose

schools.summary: The apostles commanded to preach and testify that Jesus is ordained Judge of the living and the dead (cf. Matthew 28:19-20); 'quick and dead' meaning all men — the living caught up at his coming (1 Thessalonians 4:16-17, Barnes) and the dead; Judge of Jew and Gentile alike (Robertson).
Calvin: the kingdom of Christ in his being Judge — the government of heaven, earth, and the church due to him; the 'testify' carrying weight against man's unbelief of the coming judgment.

themes: he commanded us to preach and testify
ordained to be the Judge of quick and dead

schools.summary: All the prophets witness that through Christ's name whosoever believes receives remission of sins — the way of salvation by faith, drawing no race distinctions: Gentiles need not become Jews but only believe (Robertson, Ellicott), glorious news to Cornelius.

Calvin: three points — Christ's office to reconcile by remission of sins, the reception of it by faith, and the witness of all the prophets since the beginning; regeneration comprehended under faith as its effect.

themes: to him give all the prophets witness
the gospel offered to the Gentiles

schools.summary: While Peter yet spoke, the Spirit fell on all who heard — the 'Gentile Pentecost,' God's attestation that the Gentiles were received; the timing (before baptism, before laying on of hands) marking sovereign anticipatory grace, necessary to convince Peter and the brethren the door was open (Robertson).
Henry: the sovereign, anticipatory grace of God — the Spirit confirming the gospel of faith (v.43), those who believed receiving the Spirit at once.

themes: while Peter yet spake, the Holy Ghost fell

schools.summary: The Jewish believers with Peter astonished that the Spirit fell on the Gentiles — for they held the divine influence could not reach Gentiles (Clarke, Robertson); Luke dwells on their amazement as unimpeachable testimony (Ellicott), the fulfillment of Peter's vision, the witnesses who would silence the church's objections (11:18).
Calvin: the believers' lingering error and God's patience — how hard it is to abandon long-held prejudices, even for the faithful.

themes: they of the circumcision were astonished
the gift poured out on the Gentiles also

schools.summary: The evidence of the gift was speaking with tongues and magnifying God — the same sign as at Pentecost (2:4, Robertson); they had new hearts as well as new tongues, their confession unto salvation (Clarke).
Calvin: the tongues given both for necessity (the gospel to the nations) and as an ornament to the gospel, later abused (1 Corinthians) and withdrawn when corrupted.

themes: they heard them speak with tongues
God's public seal on the Gentiles

schools.summary: Peter argued none could forbid water to those who received the Spirit 'as well as we' — the gift the indisputable warrant for baptism (the greater bestowed, the lesser not withheld, Robertson); the exceptional order (Spirit before water) showing God's gifts may flow otherwise, yet without superseding obedience to Christ's appointed baptism (Ellicott).
Calvin: reasoning from the thing to the sign — those God has testified to be his children are to receive baptism; used for infant baptism and against the Papists who tie grace to the signs.

themes: received the Holy Ghost as well as we
the Spirit the warrant for baptism

schools.summary: Peter commanded the Gentiles baptized in the name of the Lord (using the brethren, not baptizing himself, to prevent cavil, Barnes, Ellicott, cf. 1 Corinthians 1:14-17); the epochal moment — the church now of Jews and Gentiles, the middle wall broken (Clarke); the 'tarrying' showing fellowship, Peter eating freely with the converts (11:3).
Calvin (with Clarke): the apostles content with water alone in baptism, against the later Papist additions; the Gentiles received into the same grace as the Jews.

themes: commanded them to be baptized in the name of the Lord
they prayed him to tarry certain days
the church of Jews and Gentiles
